intro:
Machpelah (meaning double cave or portion) was the cave and field Abraham purchased from Ephron the Hittite at Hebron for four hundred shekels of silver — the first piece of land the patriarchs owned in Canaan (Genesis 23). The transaction is recorded in unusual legal detail, reflecting ancient Near Eastern land-purchase customs, and it serves as a tangible down payment on the promised land before the full inheritance was received. Abraham buried Sarah there; the cave subsequently became the burial place of Abraham himself, Isaac, Rebekah, Leah, and Jacob (Genesis 23:19; 25:9; 49:31; 50:13).

The site is identified with the modern Haram el-Khalil in Hebron, where a massive Herodian enclosure built over the traditional tomb of the patriarchs still stands. It is one of the holiest sites in Judaism and is venerated in Islam as the tomb of Ibrahim. The patriarchal connection to this specific location — purchased at full price before the conquest — gives Machpelah unique covenantal significance as the one piece of Canaan the patriarchs possessed in their lifetime.
